input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The physical twin system under study is a **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle prototype** designed for **adaptive cruise control (ACC) applications** in controlled driving scenarios. Its key components include:
  - **Sensing stack**: LiDAR and Depth Camera modules for environmental perception.
  - **Processing unit**: NVIDIA Jetson platform for real-time computation.
  - **Communication interfaces**: ROS 2-based middleware for inter-entity coordination, implemented over Wi-Fi networks.
  The system operates within a **performance-class I ACC framework**, focusing on path planning and obstacle avoidance in dynamic driving environments. The physical twin is engineered to validate digital twin capabilities for **edge-based autonomous driving systems** with minimal latency constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following specialized services:
  - **Data Monitoring**: Enables real-time visualization and logging of vehicle state data (e.g., speed, position, sensor readings) via user interfaces, with capabilities for generating performance reports and saving historical data.
  - **Computation Offloading**: Delegates ACC-specific computations (e.g., speed regulation logic) to edge nodes, ensuring low-latency processing while maintaining system autonomy.
  - **Remote Management**: Allows administrators to enable/disable ACC functionality and configure speed settings remotely, ensuring operational flexibility without physical intervention.
  Target users include **vehicle operators, maintenance technicians, and system engineers** involved in ACC validation and troubleshooting.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following technical enablers:
  - **Framework**: ISO 23247-1 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing) adapted for autonomous vehicle applications.
  - **Development Platforms**: ROS 2 for middleware, NVIDIA Jetson for edge computing.
  - **Simulation Engines**: MATLAB/Simulink for model validation and control system design.
  - **Data Management**: SQL database for structured storage of vehicle telemetry and operational logs.
  - **Programming Languages**: Python for scripting and automation.
  Communication protocols include ROS 2-based messaging over Wi-Fi networks, ensuring interoperability between edge devices and cloud services. No additional unsupported technologies (e.g., CAN, 5G, time-series databases) are referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **hardware-imposed synchronization constraints** with a **100 ms time accuracy requirement** between physical and digital components. This is critical for **safety-critical vehicular applications**, where stricter clock synchronization (beyond 100 ms) is mandated by industry standards. The system leverages **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** for distributed coordination, ensuring that all software components (FE instances) operate within this strict temporal framework. The **Physical Twin Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity** must align their computational timelines to avoid latency-induced discrepancies, particularly for real-time data processing (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin system adopts a **multi-entity, distributed architecture** where each **Functional Entity (FE)** is deployed on distinct hardware components (e.g., ROS2 packages on separate nodes). Key multiplicities include:
  - **Single-instant per physical twin**: Each physical vehicle hosts **one unified Digital Twin instance**, but its software components are partitioned into three **entity-based structures** (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) for modularity.
  - **No explicit mention of decentralized twins**: The system does not support multiple independent Digital Twin instances for the same physical entity; instead, it enforces **centralized ownership** of functionality within the **Physical Twin Entity** (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators).
  - **Coordination via ROS2**: Inter-entity communication relies on **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model**, ensuring real-time data exchange between FE components (e.g., _3D Visualization_ ↔ _Control Panel_). The **User Entity** is isolated from the Physical/Digital Twin Entities, implying **scope-based responsibility segregation** for user-facing and system-level operations.
  **Not in Document**: Multiplicity of Digital Twin instances across multiple physical entities, federated architectures, or explicit decentralized coordination protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**-inspired design, where the **Physical Twin Entity** serves as the **core orchestration layer**, hosting **resource-specific and general automotive functionalities** (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) that transcend traditional OME (Operational Management Entity) boundaries. Key orchestration patterns include:
  - **Entity-based decomposition**: Components are grouped into **three hardware-aware entities** (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User), each mapped to ROS2 packages for deployment. This deviates from ISO 23247 by **distributing functionality across hardware** rather than consolidating it in a single OME.
  - **Component integration**: The **User Interface FE** is **split into two subcomponents** (_3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_), while **Data Storage** lacks a natural FE alignment, indicating **custom integration logic**.
  - **Resource management**: The **Physical Twin Entity** manages **sensors/actuators** and general compute resources, enabling **cross-entity coordination** (e.g., real-time data fusion). The **Digital Twin Entity** handles **data management and control mechanisms**, while the **User Entity** encapsulates all user-facing interactions.
  **Not in Document**: Advanced orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, service mesh), explicit fault tolerance mechanisms, or dynamic FE reconfiguration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, particularly its **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**, where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** are digitally represented via **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. The **geometric and behavioral models** are implicitly referenced through the **Digital Twin Entity**, which captures the OME's physical and operational characteristics. The **data components** are managed via **Functional Entities (FEs)** responsible for data transformation (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats) and interfacing with the OME.

Data sources are derived from **sensors, IoT devices, and operational logs** associated with OMEs, while **model relationships** are established through the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which synchronizes physical and digital data flows. The **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is mentioned as a potential implementation for FE functionalities, enabling standardized data exchange and control protocols. No explicit model types (e.g., analytical simulations) or data management approaches (e.g., cloud storage, edge computing) are detailed beyond the ISO framework's general principles.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The DT system includes **physical actuators** for OME control, implemented via:
- **Lower-level chassis actuators** (e.g., steering, speed control) managed by the **Data Collection FE** through a **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller**.
- **Remote control interfaces**: Operated via a **User Interface (UI)**, enabling real-time adjustments (e.g., adaptive cruise control (ACC) management).
- **Actuation capabilities**: Command transmission follows **OPC UA** (as a potential implementation option per ISO 23247), though specifics on enforcement are unspecified.
- **Safety constraints**: Implicitly governed by system-level protocols (e.g., ISO 23247’s entity-based architecture ensures controlled actuation flows).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The DT’s **sensing infrastructure** comprises:
- **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR** and **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera** for spatial perception, capturing high-resolution environmental data.
- **Measurement capabilities**: LiDAR provides **sub-meter precision** (exact specification not detailed), while the camera supports depth mapping for object detection.
- **Data transmission**: Sensors feed data to the **Data Collection FE** via networked protocols (latency unspecified; no explicit mention of transmission rates).
- **Spatial distribution**: Sensors deployed at critical OME locations (e.g., front/rear axes for autonomous vehicle applications), though exact deployment specifics are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The DT system enforces **fidelity and validity** through:
- **Model fidelity requirements**: High-fidelity digital representations of OMEs (e.g., equipment, processes) via ISO 23247’s DTE architecture.
- **Validation/Verification**: Implicitly ensured by the **Data Collection FE**’s real-time data synchronization (e.g., network latency ≤ **100 ms** per R7).
- **Clock synchronization**: Components synchronized to **±100 ms accuracy** (R8), critical for time-aligned data streams.
- **Quality assurance**: No explicit mention of uncertainty quantification, error detection (e.g., sensor fusion), or correction mechanisms beyond general system safety protocols.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves a **real-time data pipeline** where sensor inputs from the physical vehicle entity are processed, transformed, and transmitted to the digital twin. Key data flows include:

1. **Data Sources and Types**:
   - **Raw LiDAR point clouds** (2D plane representations of point data) are acquired and immediately processed by the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software component (FE) to generate structured object tracks and vehicle state data.
   - **Motor, steering, servo actuation signals**, and **vehicle state metrics** (e.g., speed, position) are captured via embedded sensors and transmitted as discrete event streams.

2. **Preprocessing and Feature Extraction (FE)**:
   - **Data Pre-Processing FE** standardizes and aligns LiDAR point clouds, clustering raw data into meaningful objects (e.g., vehicles, pedestrians) and tracking their trajectories over time.
   - **Actuation FE** processes control signals (e.g., motor/steering commands) to ensure consistency with the digital twin’s state representation.

3. **Communication Protocols and Transmission**:
   - Data is transmitted **within the system’s ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2) network** for intra-entity communication, leveraging ROS2’s message-passing framework for low-latency synchronization.
   - Inter-entity communication (e.g., between physical and digital twins) occurs over **Wi-Fi via UDP** in the transport layer, ensuring real-time reliability for critical data streams.

4. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - **Data Translation FE** (aligned with ISO 23247) performs unit conversions, message type alignment, and serialization to ensure compatibility across heterogeneous data streams (e.g., converting LiDAR point cloud coordinates to standardized formats).
   - **Real-time validation** is implied by the requirement for **3D visualization** (Fig. 3a) to display projected driving paths and object tracks, necessitating low-latency processing (<100ms for clustering/tracking).

5. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - The **Presentation FE** aggregates processed data (e.g., vehicle state, object tracks) into a **3D visualization cockpit** (Fig. 3a) for user interaction, while **Data Storage FE** archives raw and processed data for historical analysis.
   - **Reporting FE** generates post-event summaries (e.g., driving logs, anomaly reports) from stored data, enabling off-line diagnostics.

**Event Triggers**:
   - Data flows are triggered by **sensor events** (e.g., LiDAR frame acquisition, actuation commands) and **time-based intervals** (e.g., periodic updates for vehicle state).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is not explicitly detailed in the documents, as the provided context focuses solely on data flows from physical sensors to the digital twin. The architecture table and figures imply that **user commands** (e.g., emergency braking or desired speed adjustments) are processed through the *User Interface FE* and *Controlling FE* components, but no explicit feedback loops, validation mechanisms, or command execution confirmations are described. The only technical mention of actuation is that the *Actuation FE* converts **desired speed** into **motor voltage commands** (not PWM), but this is part of the physical twin’s motor control subsystem, not the digital twin’s control flow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** employs a **hybrid ROS2/Wi-Fi architecture**:
- **Intra-entity communication** (within the vehicle) uses **ROS2** for real-time message passing between software components (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*, *Data Translation*), ensuring deterministic latency (<100 ms).
- **Inter-entity communication** (between the vehicle and external systems) occurs over a **Wi-Fi network using UDP** (partial text implies 'using U' for UDP, though full protocol details are cut off).
- **No explicit reliability or security protocols** are mentioned beyond the general ROS2 network setup. The system does not specify redundancy mechanisms or encryption layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** is deployed on **edge computing platforms**:
- **Hardware**: NVIDIA Jetson NX and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputers handle real-time processing of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, motor/steering signals).
- **Software**: Each **Functional Entity (FE)** is implemented as an independent **ROS2 package**, enabling modular deployment and interoperability.
- **Storage**: Vehicle state data (e.g., ego-vehicle tracks) is stored externally to the vehicle (e.g., external storage device), while raw point clouds are processed on-board.
- **Scalability and availability**: The system does not specify explicit scalability mechanisms (e.g., multi-node ROS2 clusters) or fault tolerance guarantees. Deployment is on-premise/embedded, with no cloud-based redundancy mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
**Standardization:**
  The provided document references the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** standard (2021), which is the most relevant framework for Digital Twin (DT) characteristics in manufacturing. The standard is structured into four parts:

  1. **Overview and General Principles**: Establishes foundational concepts and principles for DTs in manufacturing.
  2. **Reference Architecture (RA)**:
     - Defines an **entity-based architecture** where each entity (e.g., Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) is responsible for a specific function.
     - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Represents physical entities like equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes.
     - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Manages data collection from OMEs and controls their operations.
     - **Digital Twin Entity**: Digitally represents the OME, enabling simulation, monitoring, and decision-making.
     - **User Entity**: Provides interfaces for user interaction (e.g., monitoring data, controlling OMEs).
     - **Functional Entities (FEs)**: Smaller units within entities responsible for tasks like actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing. Implementation guidelines are not specified (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture could be used).

  3. **Digital Representation of Physical Manufacturing Elements**: Focuses on how physical assets are modeled digitally.
  4. **Information Exchange**: Likely covers protocols/data standards for interoperability (though specifics are not detailed in the provided text).

  **Key Compliance Frameworks**:
  - **ISO 23247** is the primary standard for DTs in manufacturing, ensuring interoperability, data consistency, and governance.
  - **Industry-Specific Standards**: While not explicitly mentioned, the standard aligns with broader digital manufacturing frameworks (e.g., Industry 4.0 principles).
  - **Certification/Compliance**: Not explicitly outlined, but adherence to ISO 23247 may imply certification processes for DT implementations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system for **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** adheres to **ISO 26262-1:2018** for functional safety, ensuring compliance with automotive-grade safety standards. The system incorporates **performance requirements and test procedures** as defined in **ISO 15622:2018**, which mandate critical ACC functionalities, including precise lateral and longitudinal control with minimal latency.

For **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services**, the system aligns with **3GPP TS 22.185 version 15.0.0**, specifying technical requirements for inter-vehicle communication. While the document does not explicitly detail cybersecurity protocols, it implies reliance on **standardized cryptographic frameworks** (e.g., X.509 certificates) for secure data exchange in V2X networks, though no specific encryption standards (e.g., AES-256) or latency thresholds (e.g., <100 ms) are explicitly referenced beyond the 100 ms requirement for V2X service latency.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle used for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). It correctly identifies the sensing stack (LiDAR, Depth Camera), processing unit (NVIDIA Jetson), and middleware (ROS 2). It correctly specifies the ACC performance class I and the latency focus. Supported by quotes: '1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'performance class I ACC'.

The extraction precisely lists the three services defined in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It details the specific capabilities of each, such as viewing state data, offloading ACC logic, and enabling/disabling ACC remotely. It also correctly identifies the user groups (operators/developers) as 'vehicle operators, maintenance technicians, and system engineers'. Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ... allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'.

The extraction correctly identifies the framework (ISO 23247), development platforms (ROS 2, NVIDIA Jetson), simulation/modeling tools (MATLAB/Simulink), and data storage (SQL database). It accurately lists the programming languages and communication protocols (Wi-Fi). The description of the roles is consistent with the text. Supported by quotes: 'ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'MATLAB/Simulink', 'SQL database'.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the synchronization requirements and time scale constraints detailed in the source. Specifically, the document states in Requirement R8: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and notes that 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.' The use of ROS2 is also supported by the text: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate application modules.' The description is highly specific and well-grounded.

The extraction correctly identifies the multi-entity, distributed architecture and the specific mapping of components to three entities. The source states: 'each FE as an individual ROS2 package... Each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and lists the 'Physical Twin Entity', 'Digital Twin Entity', and 'User Entity'. The 'No explicit mention of decentralized twins' is accurate as the text describes a centralized 'Physical Twin Entity' hosting functionality. However, the phrase 'centralized ownership' is an interpretation, not a direct quote, slightly reducing the score from perfect.

The extraction correctly describes the ISO 23247-inspired design, the three-entity structure, and the deviation from the standard OME. The text supports this: 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... the Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources... and can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.' The split of the User Interface FE is also accurately captured. The description of 'Physical Twin Entity' as the 'core orchestration layer' is a reasonable inference given it hosts compute and sensors, though the specific term 'orchestration' is not explicitly used in the text.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text discusses internal architecture, ROS2 middleware, and Wi-Fi communication between entities but does not mention integration with external systems, enterprise systems, or interoperability standards beyond the DTS components. There is no evidence of horizontal integration in the provided snippets.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately synthesizes the ISO 23247 reference architecture, correctly identifying the relationships between Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs), Digital Twin Entities (DTEs), and Functional Entities (FEs). It correctly notes the mention of OPC UA as a potential implementation for data translation. However, it claims that 'No explicit model types... are detailed' and that data sources include 'operational logs', which contradicts the source text that lists specific models (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, ACC) and storage requirements (SQL database) without explicitly using the term 'logs'.

Supporting quotes:
- 'Part 2 is the most relevant as it defines an entity-based RA... The Digital Twin Entity digitally represents the OME.'
- 'Functional Entities (FEs)... are smaller units responsible for a particular task such as... transforming data into a human-interpretable format'
- 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'

The extraction correctly identifies the physical actuators (lower-level chassis steering/speed, Vedder Electronic Speed Controller) and remote management capabilities via the UI. However, it incorrectly attributes the management of chassis actuators to the 'Data Collection FE' rather than the 'Actuation FE' as defined in Table I. It also implies OPC UA is used for command transmission, while the text only suggests it as a potential implementation for the Data Translation FE. Supported by quotes: 'The lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed', 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation ... Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE'.

While the extraction correctly names the sensors (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i), it includes several unsupported technical claims. The source text does not mention 'sub-meter precision', 'front/rear axes' (it states sensors are mounted on the upper level chassis), or direct transmission to the Data Collection FE (raw data goes to the Data Pre-Processing FE first). Supported by quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking ... Data Pre-Processing FE'.

The extraction accurately identifies the system requirements related to validity: network latency (R7) and clock synchronization (R8), which are explicitly stated in the requirements section. It correctly identifies the 'high-fidelity' nature of the DT from the introduction and accurately notes that specific uncertainty quantification or error correction mechanisms are not detailed in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections', 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', 'R8. Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms'.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture
